coredata.dc:title: Employing carbon quantum dots to combat cytomegalovirus
coredata.dc:creator: Bulat, Tanja
Zmejkoski, Danica
Marković, Zoran
Satzinger, Melissa
Kovač, Janez
Strobl, Birgit
Milivojević, Dušan
Todorović Marković, Biljana
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are well known as a promising therapeutic agent due to their excellent pro-oxidant, antioxidant, antibacterial and anticancer properties under visible light irradiation. This material is easy to produce by several bottom-up or top-down methods. In this study, we used citric acid as starting precursor to synthesize aqueous carbon quantum dots dispersion by pyrolysis at 210 °C. Atomic force microscopy and XPS analyses revealed that CQDs synthesized are quasi-spherical with typical diameter of 9 nm and lots of C–O and CO functional groups distributed over the basal plane and edges of the dots. Here, we investigated the antiviral properties of CQDs against cytomegalovirus (CMV). The pre-treatment with CQDs upregulates interferon-stimulated genes (ISG), resulting in better virus control. Cellular defence against cytomegalovirus of CQDs pre-treated cells is increased in a dose-dependent manner. Our results reveal high biocompatibility and potent in vitro antiviral properties of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Citric acid
Pyrolysis
Cytomegalovirus
Interferon-stimulated gene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
UV–Vis and EPR measurements of CQDs
Viral plaque formation upon CQD pre-treatment
Induction of interferon-stimulated gene expression by CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Science Fund of the Republic of Serbia, Program DIASPORA virotaxi [Grant Number 6443834 ]. Authors Biljana Todorović Marković, Zoran Marković, Danica Zmejkoski and Dušan Milivojević also thanks to the Ministry of Science, Technological Development and Innovation of the Republic of Serbia for funding [Grant numner 451-03-47/2023-01/200017 ]. Authors Tanja Bulat and Birgit Strobl acknowledge the support from the Austrian Science Fund ( FWF ) [Grant number P-34286 ]. The author Janez Kovač acknowledges the financial support from the Slovenian Research Agency [research core funding No. P2-0082 ] and Slovenian-Serbian bilateral project [Grant number BI-RS/20-21-006 ]. Special thanks to Dragana Mićović (SUPERLAB company) for FTIR measurements. Figures were created with BioRender.com .

Surface morphology of CQDs was investigated by TEM (JEOL JEM-1400) operated at 120 kV). For TEM imaging, CQDs were deposited on graphene oxide support film on lacey carbon copper grid with 400 mesh by the drop-casting method.This research was supported by the Science Fund of the Republic of Serbia, Program DIASPORA virotaxi [Grant Number 6443834]. Authors Biljana Todorović Marković, Zoran Marković, Danica Zmejkoski and Dušan Milivojević also thanks to the Ministry of Science, Technological Development and Innovation of the Republic of Serbia for funding [Grant numner 451-03-47/2023-01/200017]. Authors Tanja Bulat and Birgit Strobl acknowledge the support from the Austrian Science Fund (FWF) [Grant number P-34286]. The author Janez Kovač acknowledges the financial support from the Slovenian Research Agency [research core funding No. P2-0082] and Slovenian-Serbian bilateral project [Grant number BI-RS/20-21-006]. Special thanks to Dragana Mićović (SUPERLAB company) for FTIR measurements. Figures were created with BioRender.com.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthetic route of CQDs, (B) Top view AFM image of CQDs, (C) Particle size distribution of CQDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of fitted C1s peak of CQDs, (B) XPS spectrum of fitted O1s peak of CQDs, (C) FTIR spectrum of CQDs.

(A) UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs, (B) EPR spectrum of CQDs in dark (black curve), irradiated at ambient light for 24 h (green curve) and irradiated for 12 h by blue light (red curve). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Cytotoxicity assays of CQDs. (A) Experimental set-up. (B) Cell proliferation 24 h, 48 h and 72 h after treatment with 0.05 mg/mL CQDs. (C) Flow cytometric analysis of apoptotic cells with representative plots 24 h after treatment with CQDs. EA - early apoptosis; LA -late apoptosis. Mean ± SEM are given. For all experiments n = 4, N = 2; n-biological replicates, N-experimental repetitions.

Antiviral activity of CQDs. (A) Experimental set-up. (B) Viral titers of control, vehicle (PBS) and CQDs treated cells. Mean ± SEM are given. **p ≤ 0.001. n = 4, N = 2; n-biological replicates, N-experimental repetitions.

CQDs upregulate ISGs. (A) Experimental set-up. (B) Relative gene expression. Mean ± SEM are given. *p ≤ 0.05, **p ≤ 0.01, ***p ≤ 0.001, **p ≤ 0.0001. n = 4–6, N = 2; n-biological replicates, N-experimental repetitions.

head.ce:title: Employing carbon quantum dots to combat cytomegalovirus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Animal Breeding and Genetics, Department of Biomedical Science, University of Veterinary Medicine Vienna, 1210, Vienna, Austria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Animal Breeding and Genetics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jozef Stefan Institute, Department of Surface Engineering - F4, Jamova cesta 39, 1000Ljubljana, Slovenia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jozef Stefan Institute, Department of Surface Engineering - F4, Jamova cesta 39, 1000Ljubljana, Slovenia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are well known as a promising therapeutic agent due to their excellent pro-oxidant, antioxidant, antibacterial and anticancer properties under visible light irradiation. This material is easy to produce by several bottom-up or top-down methods. In this study, we used citric acid as starting precursor to synthesize aqueous carbon quantum dots dispersion by pyrolysis at 210 °C. Atomic force microscopy and XPS analyses revealed that CQDs synthesized are quasi-spherical with typical diameter of 9 nm and lots of C–O and CO functional groups distributed over the basal plane and edges of the dots. Here, we investigated the antiviral properties of CQDs against cytomegalovirus (CMV). The pre-treatment with CQDs upregulates interferon-stimulated genes (ISG), resulting in better virus control. Cellular defence against cytomegalovirus of CQDs pre-treated cells is increased in a dose-dependent manner. Our results reveal high biocompatibility and potent in vitro antiviral properties of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique structural, chemical, optical and biomedical properties. This material can be considered as 0dimensional nanomaterial with a lateral size less than 100 nm, consisting of sp2/sp3 carbon network and abundant different functional groups/polymer chains distributed on the basal plane and edges of CDs. These groups enable their excellent dispersibility in water and organic solvents. The main features of CDs are the following: low production costs, good chemical stability, high resistance to photobleaching, high photoluminescence, low dark cytotoxicity, good pro-oxidant and antioxidant properties, good antibacterial and anticancer properties [1,2]. CDs include graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CND), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [3].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical and possess obvious crystal lattices and functional groups on the surface, which show intrinsic state luminescence and quantum confinement effect of the CQDs’ size [4]. The presented functional groups, i.e. oxygen or amino, can contribute to modification of different properties dominantly structural and further antimicrobial. These dots have good chemical stability, high photoluminescence, low dark cytotoxicity and very good pro-oxidant, antioxidant and antibacterial properties. There are a lot of methods for CQDs preparation [2,5].

Until now, there are only a few reports concerning the antiviral potential of CQDs. Antiviral properties of CDs can be tuned by the selection of synthesis precursors and by easy post-synthetic functionalization [6]. Barras et al. showed that carbon dots (C-dots) formed from 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride (4-AB/C-dots) using a modified hydrothermal carbonization prevented herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) infection in the nanograms per millilitre concentration range (EC50 = 80 and 145 ng/mL on Vero and A549 cells, respectively). In contrast, the corresponding C-dots formed from phenylboronic acid (B/C-dots) have no effects even at high concentrations [7]. Łoczechin et al. prepared and tested seven different types of CQDs against human coronavirus HCoV-19 229E [8]. In this report, CQDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonization from ethylenediamine/citric acid as carbon precursors and postmodified with boronic acid ligands showed a concentration-dependent inhibition of viral infection with an estimated EC50 of 52 ± 8 μg/mL. CQDs prepared from 4-aminophenylboronic acid without any further modification resulted in the second generation of anti-HCoV nanomaterials with an EC50 lowered to 5.2 ± 0.7 μg/mL. The underlying mechanism of action of these CQDs was revealed to be inhibition of HCoV-229E entry that could be due to interaction of the functional groups of the CQDs with HCoV-229E entry receptors. Chen et al. synthesized curcumin-carbon quantum dots and showed their antiviral activity against Japanese encephalitis virus infection (JEV). They found that curcumin-carbon quantum dots effectively bound to the E protein of JEV, preventing viral entry into the host cells [9]. Du et al. studied the effect of carbon dots on the replication of virus by using pseudorabies virus (PRV) and porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (PRRSV) [10]. CDs can significantly inhibit the multiplication of PRV and PRRSV due to the induction of interferon-α (IFN-α) production and the expression of IFN-stimulating genes (ISGs) which inhibit virus replication. Garg et al. found that triazole functionalized heteroatom co-doped carbon quantum dots (TFH-CQDs) can synergistically exert an intensified antiviral response against human coronavirus [11]. Namely, these dots may block the viral entry by perturbing various interactions with the host cells as well as the viral enzymes such as helicase and 3CLpro, important for viral replication.

Human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) causes acute infection and establishes latency. Global seroprevalence is estimated at 83 %, with the potential of virus to reactivate [12]. Primary infection is associated with severe disease such as pneumonia, bone marrow failure, encephalitis and organ failures in immunocompromised individuals (e.g. elderly, pregnant females, transplantation patients) [13,14]. Since the virus can be transmitted from the mother to the foetus, new-borns are endangered of developing mental retardation, deafness and microencephaly [15]. While there are HCMV vaccines in clinical trials, none have been licensed yet, since immunoevasive mechanisms of the virus pose limitations to potential vaccine effectiveness [16]. Additionally, data shows emerging resistance to ganciclovir, an antiviral drug frequently given as treatment, indicating the pressing need for new antiviral treatment [17].

The murine cytomegalovirus (MCMV) is used as a model system to understand HCMV [18]. In this research, we have studied the antiviral properties of CQDs prepared from citric acid by pyrolysis against MCMV. According to our previous research, these dots have excellent antibacterial activity against wide range of bacteria especially Staphylococcus saprophyticus subsp. saprophyticus, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter aerogenes, Salmonella typhimurium, Proteus mirabilis and Klebsiella pneumonia [19]. Here, we show that pre-treatment with CQDs upregulates ISG, which reduces viral plaque formation, indicating better virus control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (10 g, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) was transferred into a porcelain boat and then heated in air at 210 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 (Fig. 1 A) [19]. The obtained carbogenic product was dissolved in water (40 mL) by use of ultrasonic tip. Solid-state carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were re-dispersed in water, filtered through 50 nm filter, dialyzed for 72 h and characterized. The pH value of CQD colloid was tuned to 7. Concentration was tuned to be 1 mg/mL. Prepared samples of CQDs were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Vis spectrophotometry and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR).

Surface morphology of CQDs was investigated by TEM (JEOL JEM-1400) operated at 120 kV). For TEM imaging, CQDs were deposited on graphene oxide support film on lacey carbon copper grid with 400 mesh by the drop-casting method.

The AFM measurements were conducted on a Quesant microscope operating in tapping mode in air at ambient temperature. CQDs samples for AFM measurements were deposited on freshly cleaved mica. Statistical analysis of all AFM images was performed in Gwyddion software [20].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was carried out on a PHI-TFA XPS spectrometer produced by Physical Electronics Inc. and equipped with Al-monochromatic X-ray source. The analysed area was 0.4 mm in diameter and the analysed depth was about 3–5 nm. The accuracy of binding energies was about ±0.5 eV. The C 1s spectrum was aligned to 284.8 eV. XPS spectra were fitted with Gauss-Lorentz functions and Shirley function was used for background removal.

FTIR measurements were performed on PerkinElmer, Spectrum Two model operated in the ATR mode. CQDs samples were deposited on Si substrates by drop casting method. Spectra were recorded at ambient temperature in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 whereas the spectral resolution was 4 cm−1.
UV–Vis spectra were recorded on LLG-UNISPEC2 spectrophotometer (LLG, Germany) in the range of 190–900 nm at ambient temperature.

EPR measurements were conducted on a Spectrometer MiniScope 300, Magnettech, Berlin, Germany. The instrument was operating at a nominal frequency of 9.5 GHz. The CQDs samples were irradiated by blue light (3W, 470 nm) for 12 h. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used as a spin trap. With 1O2, TEMP molecules quickly react and form stable, EPR active product, TEMP-1O2 (TEMPO). The samples in the concentration of 0.2 wt%, were mixed with TEMP solution in ethanol (50 % colloid 50% ethanol solution), at a concentration of 30 mM.

Primary mouse embryonic fibroblasts (pMEFs) were generated from C57BL/6N mice as previously described [21]. Briefly, from 13 to 14 days old mice embryos heads, hearts and livers were removed. Rest was minced with scissors and resuspened in PBS supplemented with penicillin (50 μg/mL) and streptomycin (50 U/mL). The suspension was filtered through a 100 μm nylon cell strainer and centrifugated at 200×g for 5 min. The pellet was washed with PBS two times, and 5 × 106 cells were plated per 10 cm dish in a complete medium containing DMEM (high glucose), 10 % FCS (heat-inactivated), Pen/Strep (100 μg/mL and 100 U/mL), 2 mM l-glutamine and 50 μM β-mercaptoethanol. Cells were split when confluent and stored in liquid nitrogen in 10 % DMSO in FCS. For all assays, pMEFs were at passage number below 4 and grown to sub-confluence. All animal experiments were approved by the institutional ethics and animal welfare committee and the national authority according to §§ 26ff. of the Animal Experiments Act, Tierversuchsgesetz 2012 - TVG 2012 (BMWFW 68.205/0173-V/3b/2019) and conform to the guidelines of FELASA and ARRIVE.
The murine cytomegalovirus (MCMV) used in this study was a tissue culture-derived (TC) Smith strain (American Type Culture Collection, ATCC® VR194).

pMEFs we seeded into 6-, 24- or 96-well plates depending on the assay. Cells were cultivated for 24 h in complete medium. Medium was replaced with fresh medium (control), medium containing 1 % PBS as vehicle control or medium containing CQDs in two different concentrations 0.05 mg/mL or 0.005 mg/mL. Cells were incubated for 24 h and processed further depending on experiments.

pMEFs were seeded into a 24-well plate in concentration of 1 × 104 cells/well. After treatment with CQDs or corresponding controls, the medium was removed and cells were infected with MCMV at three different dilutions using centrifugation enhancement (room temperature, 30 min, 110×g) followed by 90 min incubation at 37 °C, 5 % CO2. Virus was removed, and cells were overlaid with 1 % of low-melting agarose in growth medium. MCMV plaques were counted under microscope 3–4 days after infection.

Cells were seeded into flasks and treated for 24 h with CQDs. As a positive control for apoptosis, cells were treated with 1 % H2O2 for 3 h. After the treatment, cells were detached from the surface using a rubber policeman and single cell suspensions were prepared by re-suspending with a 1 mL pipet. Cells were stained with Fixable Viability Dye (APC-Cy7, working solution 1:1000 in PBS, eBioscience) and Annexin-V (BD) for 20 min at 4 °C in the dark. After washing, cells were analysed using a CytoFlex flow cytometer.

Cytotoxicity screening was performed using Sulforhodamine B colourimetric assay as previously described [22]. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well plate and after 24 h treatment with CQDs medium was replaced with DMEM and left to grow for additional 24, 48 or 72 h. Cells were fixed with ice-cold 10 % (wt/vol) trichloroacetic acid (TCA, Sigma Aldrich) for 1 h at 4 °C. After washing with water, cells were air dried, stained with 0.057% (wt/vol) Sulforhodamine B (Sigma Aldrich) for 30 min at room temperature and the unbound dye was washed with 1 % (vol/vol) acetic acid. After drying, protein-bound dye was dissolved in 10 mM Tris base solution (pH 10.5) on a gyratory shaker for 5 min. OD was measured at 510 nm in a microplate reader. Viability of cells was expressed as a percentage of the control (untreated) cells, which was set to 100 %.

RNA was isolated using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen) and reverse transcription was done using the SuperScript™ IV First-Strand Synthesis System (Invitrogen) according to manufacturer's instructions. Quantitative PCR for Stat1, Stat2, Il1b and Oas1 was done with QIAGEN assays and the Evagreen® detection system. For Ube2d2 (housekeeping gene), Tnfa and Mx1 following primers and FAM-labelled probes were used:
Ube2d2-fwd 5′-AGG TCC TGT TGG AGA TGA TAT GTT-3′,
Ube2d2-rev 5′-TTGGGAAATGAATTG TCA AGA AA-3′,
Ube2d2-probe 5′-FAM-CCA AAT GAC AGC CCC TAT CAG GGT GG-BHQ-3′.
Tnfα -fwd 5′-CAA AAT TCG AGT GAC AAG CCT G-3′,
Tnfα -rev 5′-GAG ATC CAT GCC GTT GGC-3′,
Tnfα -probe 5′-FAM-AGC CCA CGT CGT AGC AAA CCA CC-3′.
Mx1-fwd 5′-CCT GGA GGA GCA GAG TGA CAC-3′,
Mx1-rev 5′-GGT TAA TCG GAG AAT TTG GCA-3′,
Mx1-probe 5′-FAM-TTA AGG CTG GAT GAG GCT CGG CAG A-3′.
Quantitative PCR for Pkr was done with the Evagreen® detection system and following primers:
Pkr -fwd 5′-CAG TGT GAG CCC AAC TCT GA-3′,
Pkr -rev 5′-GCT GAC TGG GAA ACA CCA TT-3′.

The qPCR mixture was composed of 4 mM MgCl2, 100 nM Evagreen® or FAM probe, 1 U/rxn Hotfire Polymerase (Solis BioDyne), 200 μM dNTP mix and 1× Hotfire B buffer. PCR conditions were: 15 min at 95 °C and 40 cycles 95 °C for 20 s and 60 °C for 1 min.

The number of biological replicates (n) and experiments (N), the definition of central value and spread (mean ± SEM) and statistical significance are reported in the figure legends. Statistical analysis for PFU of MCMV was done with log-transformed data. Data were considered as statistically significant when p ≤ 0.05. P values between 0.05 and 0.1 are indicated on the graphs. Statistical analysis was performed in GraphPad Prism 9 (Student's t-test).

AFM and TEM were used to visualize the surface morphology of CQDs. Fig. 1B presents a top view AFM image of CQDs. The inset of Fig. 1B is TEM micrograph of CQDs. The shape of dots is quasi-spherical with an average diameter of 9 nm. Fig. 1C shows particle size distribution of these dots. From this figure it could be seen that almost 40% of dots had diameter of 7.5 ± 2.5 nm, whereas 20 % of dots had diameter 12.5 ± 2.5 nm. The average height of CQDs was about 1 ± 0.5 nm.

To determine the chemical composition of CQDs two methods were used: XPS and FTIR. Fig. 2 A shows fitted C1s peak of CQDs and Fig. 2B presents fitted O1s peak of CQDs. In the survey XPS spectrum of CQDs the following elements could be detected: C (77.6 at%), O (19 at%) and N (3.4 at%). The origin of nitrogen in the spectrum could be explained by the preparing procedure. Namely, citric acid as starting precursor for CQDs synthesis was pyrolyzed in the air. The C1s peak was fitted with four peaks: C1–C4 representing different bonds as follows: peak C1 at 284.8 eV assigned to C–C/C–H bonds, peak C2 at 286.0 eV assigned to C–O/C–OH/C–O–C bonds, peak C3 assigned to at 288.1 eV to CO/O–C–O/CO3 bonds, peak C4 at 288.8 eV assigned to OC–O [23,24].

The most prominent peak in C1s peak, which assigned to C–C/C–H bonds, may indicate the crystalline structure of CQDs due to presence only sp2 hybridization. Peaks O1 and O2 were identified in the O1s spectra, O1 at 531.2 eV representing OC bonds and O2 at 532.5 eV representing O–C bonds [23,24]. FTIR spectrum of CQDs is presented in Fig. 2C. The following bands could be detected: the peaks at 3386 and 2650 cm−1 could be assigned to O–H stretching vibrations from alcohol due to intermolecular bonding, whereas the peak at 2936 cm−1 originated from C–H stretching vibrations; peak at 1697 cm−1 could be assigned to CO stretching vibrations whereas the peak at 1563 cm−1 was due to CC stretching vibrations; peak at 1389 cm−1 stem from O–H bending vibrations and peak at 1201 cm−1 was due to C–O stretching vibrations; the peak at 1001 cm−1 originated from CC bending vibrations [25]. Obtained results indicate that CQDs have a honeycomb structure with CO and C–O functional groups distributed over the basal plane and edges of CQDs. FTIR results showed that some O–H groups were incorporated in aromatic rings of the honeycomb carbon network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis and EPR measurements of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To check optical properties of CQDs, absorbance measurements were conducted. UV–Vis spectrum of these dots is presented in Fig. 3 A. We could detect two absorbance bands: one at 246 nm and the other (shoulder) at 290 nm. The first peak exists due to the π–π* transition of CC and the shoulder at 290 nm corresponding to the n–π* transition of CO [26]. Fig. 3B presents the intensity of EPR signal of CQDs sample in dark for 1 h, exposed to ambient light for 24 h and irradiated to blue light for 12 h. It can be seen from this figure that CQDs sample does not produce singlet oxygen in dark. When the sample is exposed to ambient light for 24 h, the intensity of EPR signal is low. After irradiation of blue light, the CQDs sample started to produce singlet oxygen. The intensity of EPR signal increases with time reaches the maximum and after 12 h irradiation.

Following the characterisation, we aimed to determine the cytotoxicity of CQDs in pMEFs using Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay for proliferation and Annexin V flow cytometry for apoptosis (Fig. 4 A). The SRB binds stoichiometrically to proteins, and the amount of bound dye correlates to cell mass, which can be extrapolated to measure cell proliferation [22]. CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) did not influence cell proliferation at 72 h after treatment (Fig. 4B). A modest increase in cell proliferation was observed 24 h after treatment, however, it did not reach the significance threshold set. Next, we analysed cytotoxicity 24 h after CQDs treatment. Annexin V binds to phosphatidylserine on membrane surfaces, indicating early or late apoptosis depending on the integrity of the cell membrane [27]. The survival rate of CQDs-treated cells was 97.3%, which was comparable to control samples (98.7%) (Fig. 4C). We did not observe significant differences to control cells in any of stages of apoptosis. Collectively, our data indicate high biocompatibility of CQDs with pMEFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viral plaque formation upon CQD pre-treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we evaluated the antiviral properties of CQDs against MCMV using a plaque forming unit (PFU) assay (Fig. 5 A). The plaque assay specifically measures the number of infectious viral particles. Viruses are added to permissive cells and a semisolid overlay limits the spread of infection to neighbouring cells [28]. Three days after infection of pMEF, we counted number of plaques under the microscope and our results indicate strong dose-dependent antiviral activity of CQDs (Fig. 5B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Induction of interferon-stimulated gene expression by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the antiviral properties of CQDs, we isolated RNA and performed RT-qPCR from CQD-treated pMEFs (Fig. 6 A). Interferons (IFNs) have a significant role in the restriction of viral pathogens, inducing the upregulation of ISGs [29]. mRNA expression of Stat1, Stat2, Mx1, Irf7, Oas1 and Pkr was significantly upregulated upon treatment with CQDs, and the response was dose-dependent (Fig. 6B). Tumour necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) and interleukin (IL) 1 beta are important cytokines that mediate host response to inflammation/infections and contribute to virus control [30–33]. In our experiment both Tnfa and Il-1b mRNA was significantly upregulated after treatment with CQDs (Fig. 6B). Our data indicate that treatment with CQDs leads to the upregulation of ISGs and increased antiviral defense.

Antiviral responses of CQDs depended on precursors and methods used for their synthesis [6]. In this study we used CQDs prepared from citric acid as precursor by pyrolysis method. Compared to results obtained by other authors, we used a simple method for dots preparation and low-cost precursor and showed that pre-treatment with these dots induced a very good antiviral response in primary fibroblasts. We show antiviral response against MCMV, however CQDs may have similar effect on other DNA and RNA viruses [10].

In this study, we have investigated the antiviral potentials of CQDs. CQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis method. AFM, XPS and FTIR analyses showed that these dots had quasi-spherical shape with typical diameter of 9 nm and lots of C–O and carbonyl functional groups distributed over the surface and edges of CQDs. This is the first report of CQDs antiviral activity against cytomegalovirus. After pre-treatment with CQDs we observed upregulation of ISGs and a reduction of viral titers. Data indicate that CQDs synthesized from citric acid can be a promising candidate for creating a new antiviral agent against CMV.

Tanja Bulat: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Visualization, and. Danica Zmejkoski: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Zoran Marković: Methodology, and, Formal analysis. Melissa Satzinger: Formal analysis. Janez Kovač: Methodology, Data curation, and, Formal analysis. Birgit Strobl: Funding acquisition. Dušan Milivojević: Methodology. Biljana Todorović Marković: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, and, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Science Fund of the Republic of Serbia, Program DIASPORA virotaxi [Grant Number 6443834]. Authors Biljana Todorović Marković, Zoran Marković, Danica Zmejkoski and Dušan Milivojević also thanks to the Ministry of Science, Technological Development and Innovation of the Republic of Serbia for funding [Grant numner 451-03-47/2023-01/200017]. Authors Tanja Bulat and Birgit Strobl acknowledge the support from the Austrian Science Fund (FWF) [Grant number P-34286]. The author Janez Kovač acknowledges the financial support from the Slovenian Research Agency [research core funding No. P2-0082] and Slovenian-Serbian bilateral project [Grant number BI-RS/20-21-006]. Special thanks to Dragana Mićović (SUPERLAB company) for FTIR measurements. Figures were created with BioRender.com.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, A. Hu, Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications, J. Mater. Chem. C 2 (2014) 6921-6939. https://doi.org/10.1039/C4TC00988F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Treating of aquatic pollution by carbon quantum dots
Nanostructured Materials for Treating Aquatic Pollution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. M. Markovic, B. M. Todorovic Markovic, Treating of aquatic pollution by carbon quantum dots. In: Goncalves GAB, Marques P, (Eds).
